aliases: Charcoal grilled beef betel leaf rolls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Bo La Lot (grilled betel leaf beef rolls). FOODON_03302757 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bo La Lot. A0CKP (beef meat products, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bo La Lot or grilled betel leaf beef rolls.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Bo La Lot consists of 2 skewers containing 6 rolls of marinated ground beef wrapped in betel leaves, approximately 180 g, served with vermicelli noodles, fresh herbs, and fermented fish dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, minced, grilled / Bò lá lốt nutritional profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central (Ground beef, 85% lean / 15% fat, cooked, pan-broiled, NDB 21226) and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for betel leaf and Vietnamese condiments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Bo La Lot exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground beef (85% lean, cooked, pan-broiled, FDC ID 174032) as the primary protein-fat base, adjusted for betel leaf contribution (FAO/INFOODS SEA), marinade ingredients (fish sauce, sugar, garlic, lemongrass), and garnish (peanuts, fried shallots). The composite dish is approximately 65% ground beef, 10% betel leaf, 15% marinade/condiments, 10% garnish by weight. Serving weight of 180 g estimated from typical Vietnamese restaurant portion of 6 rolls on 2 skewers.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate grilled meat dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bo La Lot. The dish is predominantly ground beef (high protein, moderate fat) wrapped in betel leaves with minimal carbohydrate content (~5.2 g/100g, primarily from marinade sugars and betel leaf). GI is estimated at 30 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (30 × 5.2 × 1.80) / 100 = 2.8 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and fermented condiments (~420 mg/100g; ~756 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce or fermented dipping sauce, as the sodium content of approximately 756 mg per 180 g serving represents about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~5.2 g/100g) and low glycemic index (~30) with negligible glycemic load per serving (~2.8 g GL)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bo La Lot is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a protein-rich, low-carbohydrate option with a very low glycemic load of approximately 2.8 per serving; however, the accompanying vermicelli noodles should be portioned carefully as they contribute additional glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.8 g/100g) and cholesterol (~62 mg/100g) from ground beef, partially offset by beneficial compounds in betel leaves
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Bo La Lot in moderation due to its saturated fat content of approximately 4.8 g/100g; choosing leaner ground beef and limiting the peanut garnish can reduce total saturated fat and caloric density per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bo La Lot (Bò lá lốt) is a classic Vietnamese grilled dish consisting of seasoned minced or ground beef wrapped tightly in wild betel leaves (Piper sarmentosum, known as 'la lot') and grilled over charcoal or an open flame. The beef is typically marinated with lemongrass, garlic, shallots, fish sauce, sugar, and black pepper before being hand-rolled into small parcels and skewered. The charcoal grilling imparts a distinctive smoky, slightly charred flavour while the betel leaf releases aromatic, peppery, and mildly bitter notes that complement the savoury beef. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Vietnamese restaurants as an appetiser or main course, accompanied by rice vermicelli noodles, fresh herbs (mint, perilla, lettuce), crushed peanuts, fried shallots, and a fermented anchovy dipping sauce (mam nem). A standard serving of 2 skewers (6 rolls, approximately 180 g) provides approximately 356 kcal, 30 g protein, 22.5 g fat, and 9.4 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 2.8, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high (~756 mg per serving) due to fish sauce and fermented condiments, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. The dish is dairy-free and rich in iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 from the beef component. Betel leaves contribute small amounts of vitamin C, vitamin A, and dietary fibre. This dish is culturally significant in Vietnamese cuisine and is popular across Vietnamese diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Bo La Lot exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central ground beef (85% lean, cooked) as the primary base, with adjustments for betel leaf, marinade, and garnish components using FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data. GI is estimated by analogy with low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but direct analytical data for this composite dish is absent.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked ground beef and is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA data for betel leaf or Vietnamese condiments. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this composite dish estimation. All other -1 values are absent from the cited sources and cannot be reliably estimated without direct analytical data. Serving size of 180 g is estimated from typical Vietnamese restaurant portion of 2 skewers (6 rolls). The dish is served with vermicelli noodles and fresh herbs which are not included in the per_100g values above; the nutrient profile reflects the grilled beef rolls only. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Co Chung Restaurant) is not confirmed halal-certified; consumers should verify with the restaurant directly. GI estimated at 30 (low) by analogy with low-carbohydrate grilled meat dishes; no direct GI measurement for Bo La Lot was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026.
